seoTitle: Fix 'Domain Already in Use' Error in Google Workspace
metaDescription: Seeing 'this domain is already in use' in Google Workspace? Learn how to verify ownership with a TXT record and submit the domain recovery form correctly.
intro: The 'domain already in use' error in Google Workspace almost always means the domain is still tied to a previous Google account, blocking your new setup. This walkthrough covers filling out Google's domain recovery form, verifying ownership through a TXT record, and getting your domain freed up for a fresh Workspace account. It's based on a real fix done through Squarespace's DNS settings, but the same steps apply to most hosting providers.

steps.heading: Fill out the domain recovery form
steps.body: Open the Google Workspace support link, review the listed reasons the error can appear, and if none apply to you, submit the domain recovery form with your domain name and a valid contact email.

steps.heading: Choose the right ownership option
steps.body: Select 'No, this is my first time' if you've never signed up with this domain before, or the option to have Google contact the current owner if you believe someone else owns it.

steps.heading: Verify ownership with a TXT record
steps.body: Use the TXT record verification method rather than CNAME, since it's generally faster to set up through your domain registrar or hosting provider's DNS settings.

steps.heading: Add the TXT record and recheck
steps.body: Copy the destination target value, paste it into a new TXT record in your DNS settings, then return to the recovery form and click Check Again until verification succeeds.

steps.heading: Submit the request and confirm
steps.body: Once verification passes, submit the request and watch for a confirmation screen and follow-up email at the contact address you provided.

faq.question: Why does Google Workspace say this domain is already in use?
faq.answer: This happens in most cases because the domain is still linked to a previous Google Workspace or Google account, even if that account is inactive.

faq.question: Should I use a TXT record or CNAME to verify my domain?
faq.answer: TXT record verification is generally easier and faster to set up in your DNS settings than a CNAME record.

faq.question: What if I don't own the domain that's flagged as already in use?
faq.answer: The recovery form lets you select an option for Google to contact the current owner if you suspect someone else controls the domain.

faq.question: How long does domain verification take after adding the TXT record?
faq.answer: It can take a few attempts of clicking Check Again after adding the record, since DNS changes sometimes take a little time to propagate.
